does: This paper proposes Data-regularized Diffusion Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), a framework for aligning diffusion models with human preferences through reinforcement learning. The core contribution is using forward KL divergence to regularize the policy toward an off-policy data distribution, preventing reward hacking pathologies like quality degradation, over-stylization, and reduced diversity. The method combines reward maximization with standard diffusion loss minimization in a theoretically grounded way. Through extensive experiments on high-resolution video generation involving over a million GPU hours and ten thousand human evaluations, DDRL achieves higher human preference scores than baselines while maintaining generation quality and diversity.
connects: This work builds on the established problem of aligning generative models with human feedback through reinforcement learning, extending prior approaches that faced vulnerability to reward hacking. It responds to limitations in existing RL-based diffusion model alignment methods whose regularization mechanisms provided unreliable penalties. The paper connects to foundational work on policy regularization in RL, particularly methods using KL divergence constraints to prevent distribution shift. It also builds on the growing literature combining diffusion models with RL-based preference learning, addressing practical challenges that arose when scaling these approaches to large models and high-resolution generation tasks.
enables: This work enables more robust scaling of preference-aligned diffusion models to complex generation tasks like video synthesis. By providing a principled framework that prevents reward hacking, DDRL opens pathways for reliable human preference alignment in large-scale generative modeling without sacrificing output quality or diversity. The theoretical insights about forward KL regularization could inform alignment strategies for other generative models and RL settings. Future work could explore applying this framework to other modalities, investigating tighter integration between diffusion training and reward optimization, or developing more sophisticated regularization schemes that adapt to different reward landscapes while maintaining the reliability demonstrated here.
